name: Strawberry
tagline: The most satisfying fruit to grow at home. Sweet, fast, and perfect for pots and hanging baskets.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting. Water at the base — wet fruit causes grey mould.

sun.detail: More sun equals sweeter fruit. Shade produces leaves but few berries.

soil.detail: pH 5.5–6.5. Mix in compost. Good drainage essential.

temp.detail: Hardy in winter. Best fruit in warm days with cool nights.

seedDepth.detail: Buy starter plants — growing from seed is slow and unreliable.

tips: Remove first flowers in year one — the plant roots deeper and gives far more fruit in years two and three.
Runners (long stems with baby plants at the end) can be pegged into small pots to make free new plants.
Lay straw under the fruit to keep it off wet soil and prevent rotting.
Net the plants once berries start forming — birds will strip them overnight.
warning: Grey mould thrives in wet conditions. Space plants well and remove any rotting fruit immediately.

name: Raspberry
tagline: One of the most productive and low-maintenance fruits once established. Produces for decades.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting. Mulch well to retain moisture.

sun.detail: Full sun for best yields. Tolerates light shade.

soil.detail: Dig in plenty of compost. pH 6.0–6.5 is ideal.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. Canes die back to the ground each winter and regrow.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root canes in autumn or early spring, 5–8cm deep.

tips: After summer raspberries fruit, cut ALL canes that fruited down to the ground. Leave new green canes for next year.
Autumn varieties are even easier — cut all canes to the ground in February and they fruit on new growth.
Tie canes to wires or a frame — they flop without support and fruit rots on the ground.
Mulch heavily with wood chip each spring to suppress weeds and retain moisture.
warning: Raspberry beetle grubs inside the fruit are common. They're harmless but unpleasant — a normal part of homegrown raspberries.

name: Blueberry
tagline: Takes patience to establish but produces fruit for 20+ years. The most nutritious berry you can grow.

water.detail: Needs consistent moisture especially in the first 2 years. Use rainwater — tap water is often too alkaline.

sun.detail: Full sun essential for good fruit production and the best flavour.

soil.detail: Critical. Regular soil will slowly kill blueberries. Use ericaceous (acid) compost only.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. Needs cold winters to fruit well the following summer.

seedDepth.detail: Don't grow from seed. Plant at least two varieties near each other for cross-pollination.

tips: Always use ericaceous compost. Collect rainwater — tap water is too alkaline and will cause yellowing.
Plant at least two different varieties near each other — they cross-pollinate and produce far more fruit.
Prune out the oldest, darkest stems every winter to encourage new fruiting wood.
Mulch with pine bark or wood chip to maintain soil acidity and moisture.
warning: Yellow leaves mean the soil pH is too high — the plant cannot access nutrients. Switch to ericaceous compost and water with rainwater immediately.

name: Blackcurrant
tagline: Incredibly productive and packed with vitamin C. One bush produces kilos of fruit every summer.

water.detail: Keep moist especially during fruiting. Drought causes small, sparse berries.

sun.detail: Full sun for best yields. Tolerates partial shade but produces less fruit.

soil.detail: Dig in generous amounts of compost when planting. Top-dress with compost every autumn.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. The flowers are frost-sensitive in spring — avoid frost pockets.

seedDepth.value: Hardwood cuttings or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Take 20cm hardwood cuttings in autumn and push into the ground — they root easily.

tips: After harvesting, cut one-third of the oldest stems down to the ground — new growth produces the best fruit.
The fruiting stems are easy to strip by running your fingers down the stem over a bowl.
Blackcurrants are self-fertile — you only need one bush.
Freeze excess fruit immediately — they freeze beautifully and keep for a year.
warning: Big bud mite causes buds to swell abnormally in winter. Remove and destroy affected buds. Persistent infection means replacing the plant.

name: Redcurrant
tagline: Beautiful jewel-like clusters of tart red berries. More versatile than blackcurrant and easier to manage.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting. More drought-tolerant than blackcurrant.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade better than most fruits — a good choice for less sunny spots.

soil.detail: Not as demanding as blackcurrant. Good garden soil with some compost is sufficient.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. Can be grown as a fan against a north-facing wall.

seedDepth.value: Hardwood cuttings or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Root easily from hardwood cuttings taken in autumn.

tips: Train as a cordon (single upright stem) to save space — very productive in a narrow space.
The berries keep on the plant for several weeks after ripening — no rush to harvest.
Birds love redcurrants — net the bush as berries start to colour.
Self-fertile — one bush is enough.
warning: Sawfly caterpillars can strip leaves completely in late spring. Check weekly and remove by hand or spray with an appropriate insecticide.

name: Gooseberry
tagline: Britain's most underrated fruit. Tangy, productive, and virtually self-sufficient once established.

water.detail: Water during dry spells especially when fruits are swelling.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade better than most fruits. Good for north-facing spots.

soil.detail: Not fussy. Good garden soil with some compost works well.

temp.detail: Extremely cold-hardy. Very tough once established.

seedDepth.value: Hardwood cuttings or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Root easily from hardwood cuttings taken in winter.

care.harvest: June–July — pick green for cooking, ripe for eating

tips: Pick some berries green in June for cooking — this thins the crop and remaining berries swell to a larger size.
Train as a standard (on a single stem like a lollipop) to make harvesting easier and reduce disease.
Prune to an open goblet shape to improve airflow and reduce powdery mildew.
Self-fertile — one bush is sufficient.
warning: The thorns are vicious. Wear thick gloves when pruning and harvesting. Worth it — the fruit is exceptional.

name: Apple Tree
tagline: A long-term investment that pays dividends for decades. Even small gardens can fit a dwarf apple tree.

water.detail: Water regularly for the first 2–3 years. Established trees are largely self-sufficient.

sun.detail: Full sun essential for fruiting and disease resistance.

soil.detail: Dig in compost when planting. Mulch annually around the base.

temp.detail: Needs cold winters for dormancy. Late spring frosts can damage blossom.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root trees in autumn or winter when dormant.

tips: Choose a self-fertile variety or plant two compatible varieties nearby for pollination.
Annual pruning in winter — remove crossing branches, dead wood, and the centre to create an open goblet shape.
Thin the fruitlets in June — reduce clusters to one or two fruits for larger, better-quality apples.
Dwarf rootstocks (M9, M26) make management easy for small gardens.
warning: Codling moth and apple scab are the two main problems. Both are manageable with good hygiene — pick up fallen fruit and prune for airflow.

name: Pear Tree
tagline: More elegant than apple and often less disease-prone. Homegrown pears at peak ripeness are sublime.

water.detail: Regular watering for first 3 years. Established trees largely self-sufficient.

sun.detail: Full sun essential. Pears blossom earlier than apples — avoid frost pockets.

soil.detail: Dig in compost when planting. Deep soil important for good root development.

temp.detail: Hardy but early blossom is frost-sensitive. Avoid growing in frost pockets.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root in autumn or winter.

tips: Most pears need two compatible varieties for pollination — check the label carefully before buying.
Pears ripen off the tree — harvest when they separate easily with a gentle twist, then ripen indoors.
Fan-train against a warm wall for earlier, better-quality fruit in cooler climates.
Fireblight is the main disease — infected branches look scorched. Cut back to healthy wood immediately.
warning: Pears ripen from the inside out — a pear that feels firm on the outside may be perfect inside. Don't wait for them to feel soft on the tree.

name: Plum Tree
tagline: One of the most generous fruit trees. A mature plum can produce more fruit than you know what to do with.

water.detail: Water regularly when young. Established trees need water during dry spells when fruiting.

sun.detail: Full sun essential for the sweetest fruit.

soil.detail: Good garden soil with compost. Plums are less demanding than apples or pears.

temp.detail: Cold-hardy. Early blossom can be damaged by late frosts — avoid frost pockets.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root in autumn or winter when dormant.

tips: Many plum varieties are self-fertile — check before buying if you only have space for one tree.
Never prune plums in winter — always prune in summer to avoid silver leaf disease.
Thin heavily laden branches to prevent them snapping under the weight of fruit.
Victoria is the most reliable and popular variety for home gardens.
warning: Silver leaf disease enters through winter pruning wounds. Only prune plums, cherries, and other Prunus species in summer when sap is flowing.

name: Cherry Tree
tagline: Spectacular spring blossom followed by jewel-like fruit. Dwarf varieties make it accessible for any garden.

water.detail: Regular watering while establishing. Reduce once mature.

sun.detail: Full sun for the best fruit. Cherries need warmth to ripen properly.

soil.detail: Good garden soil. Cherries dislike waterlogged conditions.

temp.detail: Hardy but blossom is frost-sensitive. Late frosts can eliminate the crop.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root in autumn or winter.

tips: Self-fertile varieties like Stella and Sunburst remove the need for a pollination partner.
Net the tree when fruit starts to colour — birds will take every cherry otherwise.
Dwarf rootstocks (Gisela 5) keep the tree small enough for most gardens.
Only prune in summer — same as plums — to prevent silver leaf disease.
warning: Birds will strip an unprotected cherry tree in hours. Netting is not optional if you want any fruit.

name: Peach Tree
tagline: A warm, sun-ripened peach from your own garden is one of the finest things in horticulture.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist especially when fruit is swelling. Irregular watering causes fruit to split.

sun.detail: Needs maximum warmth and sun. Fan-training against a south-facing wall is ideal in cool climates.

soil.detail: Good loamy soil. Excellent drainage essential — peaches hate wet roots.

temp.value: Hardy to -15°C but needs warmth to fruit
temp.detail: Cold-hardy but needs warm summers to produce good fruit. Sheltered south-facing position essential.

seedDepth.detail: Buy a named variety as a container or bare-root tree.

tips: Fan-train against a south or west-facing wall — this is the key to reliable crops in cool climates.
Hand-pollinate the blossom with a soft brush on dry days — insects are scarce when peaches flower in early spring.
Thin fruit to one per 15cm of branch when they reach marble size — this produces much larger, better fruit.
Peach leaf curl is the main disease — hang a clear polythene rain shelter over the tree from late winter to May.
warning: Peach leaf curl (red blistered leaves) will defoliate the tree if unprotected. A simple rain shelter from January to May prevents it entirely.

name: Fig Tree
tagline: Dramatic tropical foliage and gloriously sweet fruit. Restricting the roots is the secret to fruiting.

water.detail: Water regularly when in growth. Reduce significantly in winter when dormant.

sun.detail: Needs maximum warmth and sun. South-facing wall or sheltered courtyard is ideal.

soil.value: Well-draining, moderately fertile — NOT too rich
soil.detail: Poor to moderate soil actually produces better fruit. Too rich soil causes leaves not fruit.

temp.detail: Protect young plants from hard frosts. Established plants are surprisingly cold-hardy.

seedDepth.detail: Take hardwood cuttings in winter — they root easily. Or buy a plant.

tips: Plant in a large container or restrict roots by planting in a bottomless box — this is the key to fruiting. Unrestricted roots produce leaves not figs.
Two crops per year are possible — tiny embryo figs visible in autumn are next year's main crop. Protect them from hard frosts.
In autumn, remove any large unripe figs — they won't ripen and waste the plant's energy.
Brown Turkey is the most reliable variety for cool climates.
warning: Restricting the roots is non-negotiable for fruit production. An unrestricted fig tree in rich soil will grow enormous and produce almost no fruit.

name: Grape Vine
tagline: Dramatically productive when well-pruned. Grow over a pergola for fruit, shade, and stunning autumn colour.

water.detail: Water regularly when young. Established vines are drought-tolerant. Reduce watering as grapes ripen.

sun.detail: Maximum sun and warmth essential. South-facing wall or greenhouse for reliable crops in cool climates.

soil.detail: Deep, well-draining soil. NOT rich — over-fertile soil produces leaves not grapes.

temp.detail: Many varieties are cold-hardy. Desert varieties need greenhouse protection in cool climates.

seedDepth.value: Hardwood cuttings or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Take hardwood cuttings in winter — they root reliably. Or buy a young plant.

tips: Pruning is everything with grapes — without annual hard pruning you get leaves and no fruit. Learn the Guyot or spur pruning system.
Thin grape clusters by removing some berries — this improves air circulation and increases the size of remaining grapes.
For outdoor growing in cool climates, choose varieties specifically bred for northern growing like Solaris or Regent.
Indoor greenhouse grapes (Black Hamburg, Muscat) produce reliably in almost any climate.
warning: Without proper annual pruning, a grape vine quickly becomes an unmanageable tangle that produces no fruit. Pruning is not optional.

name: Kiwi Vine
tagline: Surprisingly hardy and vigorous. Given space and sun, produces dozens of kiwi fruits every autumn.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist during growing season. Drought causes fruit drop.

sun.detail: Full sun essential. Sheltered position important — protect from strong winds.

soil.detail: Deep, fertile, well-draining soil. Feed monthly during growing season.

temp.detail: Surprisingly cold-hardy. Young plants need frost protection for first 2 winters.

seedDepth.detail: You need one male plant to every 6 female plants for pollination.

tips: You need at least one male plant to pollinate the female fruiting plants — check labels carefully.
Hardy kiwi (Actinidia arguta) produces grape-sized fruits and is even more cold-hardy — a better choice for cool climates.
Prune side shoots back to 5 leaves in summer to control vigour and encourage fruiting spurs.
Give it a very strong support structure — mature kiwi vines become extremely heavy.
warning: Needs a very robust support — a mature kiwi vine can weigh hundreds of kilograms. A weak trellis will eventually collapse.

name: Cantaloupe Melon
tagline: Challenging but the reward — a sun-warm melon from your own garden — is exceptional.

water.detail: Keep moist but reduce watering significantly as fruit ripens to concentrate the sugars.

sun.detail: Maximum sun and heat. Greenhouse or polytunnel essential in cool climates.

soil.detail: Plant into generous amounts of compost. Feed weekly with high-potassium fertiliser.

temp.detail: Demands sustained heat. A cold summer produces no ripe fruit.

seedDepth.detail: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost. Plant in the warmest spot possible.

tips: Train to a single main stem. Pinch out side shoots and allow only 3–4 fruits to develop per plant.
Support developing fruit in nets or old tights tied to the trellis.
Reduce watering significantly when fruit is nearly ripe — this concentrates the sugars dramatically.
Ripe melons smell sweet near the stem end and separate from the vine with a gentle twist.
warning: Requires sustained high temperatures to ripen properly. In cool climates, expect failure without a greenhouse.

name: Watermelon
tagline: Possible in warm summers with the right variety. A homegrown watermelon is an extraordinary achievement.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruit development. Reduce dramatically as fruit ripens.

sun.detail: Demands maximum heat and sunlight.

soil.detail: Sandy, nutrient-rich soil with excellent drainage.

temp.detail: Needs hot days and warm nights. Very long season required.

seedDepth.detail: Start indoors 4–5 weeks before last frost.

tips: Choose small-fruited icebox varieties for cool climates — they mature faster.
The hollow sound when tapped and the yellowing of the ground spot signal ripeness.
Limit to 2–3 fruits per plant for the largest, sweetest melons.
Plant through black plastic mulch to maximise soil temperature.
warning: Needs a very long hot season. Challenging even in warm climates. Choose early-maturing varieties.

name: Lemon Tree
tagline: Possible even in cool climates in a pot. The flowers smell incredible and the fruit is a real achievement.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm of soil is dry. Never waterlogged. Use room-temperature water.

sun.detail: As much sun as possible. South-facing wall or greenhouse. Outdoors in summer only in cool climates.

soil.detail: Use citrus compost. Must drain fast — never sit in water.

temp.detail: Not frost-hardy. In cool climates: outside in summer, bright cool room in winter.

seedDepth.detail: Seed-grown lemons take 7–15 years to fruit. Buy a grafted tree for fruit in 2–3 years.

tips: Feed with citrus fertiliser every month from spring to autumn — yellowing leaves usually mean nutrient deficiency.
Move outside in late May when nights are above 10°C. Move back inside in September.
Inconsistent watering causes fruit drop. Keep moisture consistent.
Dry indoor air in winter causes leaf drop — mist the leaves occasionally or use a pebble tray.
warning: Scale insects (brown lumps on stems) are the main pest on indoor citrus. Check monthly and treat early with neem oil.

name: Orange Tree
tagline: Fragrant flowers and vivid fruit in a pot. More demanding than lemons but spectacular when it works.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm is dry. Consistent moisture is key — fluctuations cause fruit drop.

sun.detail: More sun-demanding than lemons. South-facing greenhouse is ideal in cool climates.

soil.detail: Specialist citrus compost essential for correct pH and drainage.

temp.detail: Slightly less cold-tolerant than lemons. Keep above 12°C in winter.

seedDepth.detail: Always buy a grafted tree — seed-grown takes too long to fruit.

tips: Feed monthly with citrus fertiliser year-round, increasing frequency in summer.
The fragrance of the flowers is extraordinary — worth growing for the scent alone.
Calamondin orange is easier than sweet orange and produces fruit more reliably in cool climates.
Repot every 2–3 years into a slightly larger pot in spring.
warning: Needs more warmth than lemons to produce good fruit. In cool climates a heated greenhouse gives much better results than a windowsill.

name: Lime Tree
tagline: The most aromatic citrus you can grow. Even the leaves are fragrant and useful in cooking.

water.detail: Water consistently. Limes are more sensitive to drought than other citrus.

sun.detail: Needs maximum sun. More heat-demanding than lemons.

soil.detail: Specialist citrus compost. Excellent drainage essential.

temp.detail: Less cold-tolerant than lemons. Must be kept warm.

seedDepth.detail: Always buy grafted — not from seed.

tips: Kaffir lime (Citrus hystrix) is easier to grow and the leaves are the primary culinary use.
Feed monthly with citrus fertiliser.
The Persian lime (seedless) is the most productive variety for container growing.
Move outside in summer to the hottest, most sheltered spot available.
warning: More cold-sensitive than lemons. Must come indoors earlier in autumn.

name: Avocado
tagline: Growing an avocado from a pit is a satisfying houseplant project — fruiting requires patience of years.

water.detail: Water when top 3–4cm is dry. Tolerates some drought but hates waterlogging.

sun.detail: Needs maximum light. A south-facing window or conservatory.

soil.detail: Good potting compost with excellent drainage. pH 6.0–6.5.

temp.detail: Tropical plant. Not frost-hardy. Needs warm conditions year-round.

seedDepth.detail: Push 3 toothpicks into the pit and suspend over a glass of water, broad end down. Roots in 2–6 weeks.

care.spacing: Container plant — can become a large tree

tips: Growing from the pit produces a beautiful houseplant but fruiting takes 5–13 years and rarely happens outside tropical climates.
Pinch out the growing tip when 15cm tall to encourage a bushy rather than leggy plant.
Feed monthly with a balanced fertiliser during the growing season.
The real achievement is a healthy, beautiful houseplant — manage expectations about fruit.
warning: Fruiting in cool climates is extremely unlikely from a pit-grown plant. Grow it as a beautiful tropical houseplant rather than expecting fruit.

name: Passion Fruit
tagline: Extraordinary flowers and tangy tropical fruit. Surprisingly possible in warm sheltered gardens or greenhouses.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Drought causes flower and fruit drop.

sun.detail: Maximum sun and warmth. Greenhouse or very sheltered south-facing position.

soil.detail: Good compost-rich soil with excellent drainage. Feed weekly during growing season.

temp.detail: Frost-sensitive. In cool climates grow in a greenhouse or bring indoors in winter.

seedDepth.detail: Soak seeds overnight before sowing. Germination needs warmth (25°C+).

care.harvest: Fruit when skin wrinkles — 70–80 days from fruit set

tips: Hand-pollinate flowers with a soft brush — transfer pollen between flowers on the same plant for fruit set.
The fruit is ripe when the skin wrinkles — don't harvest before this.
Prune back hard after fruiting to keep the plant manageable.
P. caerulea (blue passion flower) is hardier and produces small edible fruits in warm summers.
warning: Needs sustained warmth to fruit. In cool climates, a greenhouse is required for reliable fruiting.

name: Quince Tree
tagline: Beautiful pink spring blossom, golden autumn fruit, and the most intense fragrance of any fruit tree.

water.detail: Water regularly when establishing. Drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: Full sun for the best fruit. Tolerates some shade.

soil.detail: Adaptable to most soils. Tolerates heavier clay better than most fruit trees.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy. One of the most adaptable fruit trees.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root in autumn or winter.

tips: Quince fruit is inedible raw — it must be cooked. It makes the finest jelly, membrillo (quince paste), and poached desserts.
Self-fertile — one tree is sufficient.
The fruit has such an intense fragrance that storing them in a bowl perfumes an entire room.
Fireblight can affect quince — cut back infected material to healthy wood immediately.
warning: Raw quince is astringent and unpleasant — always cook before eating. The cooked fruit is extraordinary.

name: Medlar Tree
tagline: An ancient, beautiful tree that virtually looks after itself. The fruit requires bletting (partial rotting) before eating.

water.detail: Water when establishing. Extremely drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade better than most fruit trees.

soil.detail: Exceptionally adaptable. Grows in poor, clay, or chalky soils.

temp.detail: Extremely cold-hardy. One of the toughest fruit trees.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root in autumn or winter.

tips: Blet the fruit after harvest — leave in a cool dry place until the flesh softens and turns brown (2–3 weeks). This is essential before eating.
Self-fertile — one tree is sufficient.
Beautiful twisted branching structure makes it ornamental in winter.
One of the most low-maintenance fruit trees you can grow.
warning: Freshly harvested medlars are inedible — they must be bletted until soft and brown before the flavour develops. This is not a sign of rotting.

name: Elderberry
tagline: Fragrant flower clusters for cordial in early summer, then black berries for wine, jam, and medicine.

water.detail: Water regularly for first 2 years. Extremely drought-tolerant once established.

sun.detail: Adaptable — grows in full sun or partial shade.

soil.detail: Very adaptable. Grows in almost any soil including clay.

temp.detail: Extremely cold-hardy.

seedDepth.value: Hardwood cuttings or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Hardwood cuttings in winter root easily. Or buy a small plant.

care.harvest: Flowers in June, berries in August–September
care.spacing: 3–5 m — grows into a large shrub

tips: Harvest flowers on dry days in the morning before the fragrance diminishes.
The berries must be cooked before eating — raw berries cause nausea.
Cut back hard every year or two to encourage vigorous growth and the best flowers.
Both the flowers and cooked berries have significant medicinal properties — widely used in traditional medicine.
warning: All parts except ripe cooked berries and flowers are toxic. Never eat the leaves, bark, roots, or raw berries.

name: Damson Tree
tagline: The toughest and most reliable of all stone fruits. Deep purple fruit with an intense sharp flavour for cooking.

water.detail: Water when establishing. Very drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: More shade-tolerant than other plums. Good for less sunny spots.

soil.detail: Very adaptable. Tolerates heavier, wetter soil than most fruit trees.

temp.detail: Extremely cold-hardy. More resilient than dessert plums.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root in autumn or winter.

tips: Self-fertile — one tree is sufficient.
The fruit is too sharp to eat raw but makes the finest jam, gin, and crumble of any plum.
Only prune in summer — same rule as all Prunus to avoid silver leaf disease.
One of the most reliable and low-maintenance fruit trees for cold, exposed, or difficult sites.
warning: Damsons are not for eating raw — the intense sharpness is unpleasant. Cooked or preserved, they're exceptional.

name: Mulberry Tree
tagline: A slow-growing but very long-lived tree producing extraordinary dark red-black fruit that stains everything.

water.detail: Water regularly when establishing. Drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: Full sun for the best, sweetest fruit.

soil.detail: Good garden soil. Not particularly demanding.

temp.detail: Cold-hardy once established. Young plants need frost protection.

seedDepth.detail: Very slow from seed. Buy a named container tree.

tips: Spread a sheet under the tree and shake the branches — ripe mulberries fall easily.
The juice stains clothes, paving, and skin permanently. Site carefully and wear old clothes when harvesting.
Very slow-growing — plant as a gift to the future and to future owners of the garden.
Self-fertile — one tree is sufficient.
warning: The fruit stains absolutely everything it touches — clothes, paving, walls. Position the tree accordingly and wear old clothes at harvest time.

name: Apricot Tree
tagline: The most rewarding stone fruit to grow well. Sun-warm apricots from the garden are in a different category.

water.detail: Keep moist especially when fruit is swelling. Irregular watering causes fruit to split.

sun.detail: Maximum sun and warmth. Fan-train against a south-facing wall in cool climates.

soil.detail: Good, free-draining soil. Apricots hate wet roots.

temp.value: Hardy to -20°C but needs warmth to fruit
temp.detail: The tree is cold-hardy but the early blossom is very frost-sensitive.

seedDepth.detail: Buy a named variety — self-fertile varieties like Flavorcot are best.

tips: Fan-train against a south-facing wall — this is the key to reliable crops in cool climates.
Hand-pollinate blossom with a soft brush — blossom opens in late winter when few insects are active.
Protect blossom from frost with fleece on cold nights in spring.
Thin fruit to one per 7cm of branch for the best quality.
warning: Late frosts kill the blossom and eliminate the entire crop. Protecting the blossom with fleece on cold nights is essential.

name: Nectarine Tree
tagline: A smooth-skinned peach with an even more intense flavour. Same care as peach but slightly more tender.

water.detail: Consistent moisture essential. Drought causes fruit drop and splitting.

sun.detail: Needs maximum warmth. Fan against a south-facing wall or greenhouse.

soil.detail: Good loamy soil with excellent drainage.

temp.detail: Slightly less cold-tolerant than peaches. Protected position essential.

seedDepth.detail: Buy a named variety as a container or bare-root tree.

tips: All the same care as peach — fan-training, hand pollination, thinning, and peach leaf curl protection.
Slightly more demanding than peaches — a greenhouse gives much more reliable results.
Lord Napier and Pineapple are reliable varieties for cooler climates.
Self-fertile — one tree is sufficient.
warning: Peach leaf curl affects nectarines just as badly as peaches. A rain shelter from January to May is essential.

name: Blackberry
tagline: Enormously productive and virtually indestructible. Thornless varieties make harvesting a pleasure.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting. More drought-tolerant than raspberries.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade better than most fruits. Full sun for best yields.

soil.detail: Adaptable to most soils. Not as demanding as raspberries.

temp.detail: Extremely cold-hardy. Essentially indestructible once established.

seedDepth.detail: Buy canes or propagate for free by tip layering — push a long cane tip into soil and it roots.

tips: Choose thornless varieties (Loch Ness, Waldo) — the fruit is identical and harvesting is infinitely more pleasant.
Same pruning system as raspberries — cut fruited canes to the ground after harvest, tie in new canes.
Train along wires to contain the vigorous growth and make picking easier.
Tip layering is free propagation — push a long cane tip into soil in summer, it roots within weeks.
warning: Thorned varieties are genuinely ferocious. Thornless varieties are strongly recommended for garden use.

name: Loganberry
tagline: A raspberry-blackberry hybrid with long, dark red fruit and an intense, complex flavour.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting.

sun.detail: Full sun for best yields and flavour.

soil.detail: Good compost-rich soil.

temp.detail: Cold-hardy once established.

seedDepth.detail: Same propagation as blackberry — tip layering works well.

tips: Same pruning system as raspberries and blackberries — cut fruited canes to the ground after harvest.
The flavour is more intense than either raspberry or blackberry — excellent for jam.
Less productive than blackberry but the flavour makes it worth growing.
Train along wires to manage the vigorous growth.
warning: Has thorns — not as vicious as blackberry but wear gloves when pruning.

name: Tayberry
tagline: A raspberry-blackberry cross with large, dark red fruit and exceptional flavour. Less common but superb.

water.detail: Keep moist during the growing and fruiting season.

sun.detail: Full sun for best results.

soil.detail: Good compost-rich soil.

temp.detail: Cold-hardy once established.

seedDepth.detail: Buy canes from a specialist fruit nursery.

tips: Larger and sweeter than loganberry — excellent for eating fresh as well as cooking.
Same pruning as all hybrid berries — cut fruited canes to the ground after harvest.
Produces over a longer period than raspberries alone.
Thorned — wear gloves when working with the plants.
warning: Less widely available than other berries — order from specialist fruit nurseries.

name: Jostaberry
tagline: A blackcurrant-gooseberry cross with large, sweet-tart berries and none of the gooseberry thorns.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting. Reasonably drought-tolerant.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade well.

soil.detail: Good garden soil with some compost.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy and disease-resistant.

seedDepth.value: Hardwood cuttings or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Roots easily from hardwood cuttings taken in autumn.

tips: Completely thornless — much easier to harvest than gooseberry.
More disease-resistant than blackcurrant — rarely affected by big bud mite or mildew.
Self-fertile — one plant is sufficient.
The large berries can be eaten fresh when fully ripe, unlike blackcurrant.
warning: Non-toxic. One of the most problem-free soft fruits you can grow.

name: Honeyberry
tagline: The earliest fruiting berry — ready before strawberries. Elongated blue fruit with a blueberry-raspberry flavour.

water.detail: Keep moist. Reasonably drought-tolerant once established.

sun.detail: Full sun for best yields.

soil.detail: pH 5.5–7.0. More adaptable than blueberry.

temp.detail: Extraordinarily cold-hardy. One of the most frost-tolerant fruit plants.

seedDepth.detail: Need at least two different varieties for cross-pollination.

care.harvest: May–June — the earliest fruit of the year

tips: One of the very first fruits of the year — ready weeks before strawberries.
Must plant at least two different varieties for pollination.
More adaptable than blueberry — tolerates a wider pH range and colder conditions.
The early fruit makes it invaluable for hungry gap gardening.
warning: Two varieties are required for pollination — a single plant will produce very little or no fruit.

name: Goji Berry
tagline: The superfruit you can grow in your own garden. Incredibly hardy and produces for decades.

water.detail: Drought-tolerant once established. Don't overwater.

sun.detail: Full sun for the best fruit production.

soil.detail: Prefers slightly alkaline soil. Very adaptable overall.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy perennial shrub.

seedDepth.value: Seeds 0.5cm deep or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Grows readily from seed or buy a young plant for faster fruiting.

tips: Grows vigorously and can spread by suckers — plant in a contained area or pot.
Berries must be ripe (bright red) before harvesting — underripe berries are bitter.
Can be trained as a hedge or left to grow as an informal shrub.
Dried goji berries from the supermarket can be planted — soak overnight and the seeds germinate easily.
warning: Can spread aggressively via underground suckers. Consider growing in a large container to contain it.

name: Sea Buckthorn
tagline: Vivid orange berries packed with vitamins. Incredibly tough — thrives in coastal, exposed, or sandy conditions.

water.detail: Extremely drought-tolerant once established. Essentially self-sufficient.

sun.detail: Full sun essential.

soil.value: Any well-draining soil, even poor sandy
soil.detail: Thrives in poor, sandy, coastal soil where little else grows.

temp.detail: Extraordinarily cold-hardy.

seedDepth.detail: Need one male plant to every 6 female plants. Only female plants produce berries.

tips: You need both male and female plants — only females fruit, males just pollinate.
The berries are very difficult to harvest — the stems are covered in thorns. Freeze the whole branch and strip frozen.
The berries are too tart to eat raw for most people but make excellent juice, jam, and oil.
Excellent windbreak and coastal garden plant.
warning: Very thorny — wear thick gloves when harvesting or pruning. The freezing harvesting technique is strongly recommended.

name: Rhubarb
tagline: Plant once, harvest for 20+ years. Enormous productive plants with reliable stems every spring.

water.detail: Water regularly in dry spells during the growing season. Very drought-tolerant otherwise.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade better than most vegetables.

soil.detail: Dig in huge amounts of compost at planting. Top-dress with compost every autumn.

temp.detail: Needs cold winters to break dormancy properly. Very cold-hardy.

seedDepth.detail: Plant crowns in spring with the growing bud just at or slightly above soil level.

care.germination: Buy crowns — don't grow from seed
care.harvest: Don't harvest year 1. Then April–June from year 2
care.spacing: 90 cm — they spread to a large clump

tips: Don't harvest a single stem in the first year — let the plant establish fully.
Forced rhubarb (covering with a bucket in February) produces tender, sweeter, pink stems 3–4 weeks earlier.
Remove flower stalks immediately — flowering weakens the plant.
Split the crown every 5–6 years to maintain vigour and create new plants.
warning: The leaves are highly toxic — oxalic acid levels are dangerous. Only ever eat the stems. Never put leaves in cooking or eat them.

name: Cape Gooseberry
tagline: Golden berries wrapped in papery lanterns. Sweet-tart flavour and very ornamental.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Drought causes fruit to be small and sparse.

sun.detail: Full sun for the best fruit production.

soil.detail: Good potting compost. Not too rich — over-feeding produces leaves not fruit.

temp.detail: Treat as a half-hardy annual in cool climates. Start indoors and move out after last frost.

seedDepth.detail: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost.

care.harvest: August–October — when papery husk turns brown

tips: Harvest when the papery husk turns brown and papery — the fruit inside will be golden.
Can be overwintered indoors as a perennial in cool climates — bring in before first frost.
Stake the plants — they become large and top-heavy.
The fruit keeps for weeks inside the papery husk — better storage than most soft fruits.
warning: The unripe fruit and all green parts are toxic. Only eat fruit when the husk is fully brown and papery and the berry inside is golden.

name: Cranberry
tagline: A ground-covering acid-loving berry that produces the famous tart red fruit used in sauces and juices.

water.detail: Keep consistently moist. Cranberries love water — they naturally grow in boggy conditions.

sun.detail: Full sun for best fruit production.

soil.detail: Ericaceous (acid) compost is essential. Cranberries need acidic, moist, peaty conditions.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy perennial.

seedDepth.detail: Buy young plants and plant in large containers of ericaceous compost.

tips: Grow in large containers of ericaceous compost — acidic conditions are non-negotiable.
Keep consistently moist — cranberries love water.
Use rainwater for watering — tap water is too alkaline.
Can be grown as an attractive evergreen ground cover.
warning: The extremely acidic, moist soil requirements make cranberries challenging in normal garden conditions. Containers with ericaceous compost are the most reliable approach.

name: Boysenberry
tagline: A large, dark, complex-flavoured hybrid berry — raspberry, blackberry, and loganberry in one fruit.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting season.

sun.detail: Full sun for the best yields.

soil.detail: Good compost-rich soil.

temp.detail: Cold-hardy once established.

seedDepth.detail: Buy canes from a specialist fruit nursery.

tips: Same pruning system as all hybrid berries — cut fruited canes to the ground after harvest.
The large berries have a complex, wine-like flavour — excellent for jam and desserts.
Less widely available than blackberry or raspberry — order from specialist nurseries.
Thornless varieties are available and recommended.
warning: Less widely available in Europe than in the US and New Zealand — may need to order from specialist fruit nurseries.

name: White Currant
tagline: The mildest and sweetest of the currants. Translucent white berries that look beautiful and taste delicate.

water.detail: Keep moist during fruiting. More drought-tolerant than blackcurrant.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade well.

soil.detail: Good garden soil with some compost.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy.

seedDepth.value: Hardwood cuttings or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Root easily from hardwood cuttings taken in autumn.

tips: Sweeter and milder than red or blackcurrant — can be eaten fresh from the bush.
White Versailles and White Pearl are the most commonly available varieties.
Same care and pruning as redcurrant.
Birds find them less attractive than red berries — slightly less netting required.
warning: Non-toxic. One of the least problematic soft fruits to grow.

name: Hardy Banana
tagline: Surprisingly cold-hardy tropical statement plant. Enormous leaves and dramatic presence in any garden.

water.detail: Thirsty plant with huge leaves. Keep consistently moist in growing season.

sun.detail: Full sun and a sheltered spot for the best growth.

soil.detail: Very fertile, moisture-retaining soil. Feed generously in summer.

temp.detail: The pseudostem dies back in frost but the roots survive. Mulch heavily in winter.

seedDepth.value: Plant offsets or buy a plant
seedDepth.detail: Separate offsets from the base of established plants.

care.harvest: Rarely produces edible fruit outside tropical climates

tips: Cut back the main stem to 1m in autumn and wrap with fleece or straw for winter protection.
New shoots emerge from the base each spring — the plant regrows vigorously.
Grown primarily as an ornamental in cool climates — the fruit rarely ripens outside the tropics.
Creates an instant tropical atmosphere — spectacular combined with other exotic plants.
warning: Not grown for edible fruit in cool climates — grown as a dramatic ornamental. Manage expectations accordingly.

name: Feijoa
tagline: An underappreciated evergreen shrub with beautiful flowers and pineapple-guava flavoured fruit.

water.detail: Drought-tolerant once established. Water regularly when young and during fruiting.

sun.detail: Full sun for the best fruiting.

soil.detail: Adaptable to most soils with good drainage.

temp.detail: Surprisingly cold-hardy for a subtropical plant.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds germinate readily. Buy named varieties for reliable fruit.

tips: The fruit is ripe when it falls naturally from the tree — don't harvest early.
The red and white flower petals are edible and delicious — sweet and fruity.
Plant two or more for better pollination, or choose a self-fertile variety.
Makes an excellent ornamental evergreen hedge as well as a fruiting shrub.
warning: Fruit that falls early before naturally ripe will be astringent and unpleasant. Wait for natural fruit fall.

name: Pomegranate
tagline: Stunning orange-red flowers and dramatic fruit. Surprisingly hardy and grows well as a container plant.

water.detail: Water regularly during the growing season. Drought-tolerant once established.

sun.detail: Needs maximum sun and heat for fruit to ripen.

soil.detail: Sandy, well-draining soil preferred. Not too rich.

temp.detail: More cold-hardy than most tropical fruits. Container growing allows overwintering indoors.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds from fresh pomegranate germinate readily. Or buy a named fruiting variety.

tips: Container growing allows moving to a sunny sheltered spot in summer and overwintering in a frost-free place.
The Wonderful variety is the most widely grown for fruit quality.
Even in cool climates the spectacular flowers make it worth growing ornamentally.
Fruit ripens very slowly — needs 6–7 months of warm weather from flower to ripe fruit.
warning: Fruit production requires a long hot summer. In cool climates, the flowers are the main reward — fruit ripening is unlikely without greenhouse protection.

name: Pawpaw
tagline: North America's largest native fruit. Custard-like flesh with a tropical banana-mango flavour.

water.detail: Water regularly while establishing. Drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade when young. Full sun for best fruiting in maturity.

soil.detail: Deep, rich, moist soil. Slightly acidic pH preferred.

temp.detail: Surprisingly cold-hardy. Native to the eastern United States.

seedDepth.detail: Seeds need 60–90 days cold stratification before germination. Or buy young plants.

care.spacing: 3–5m — plant at least two for pollination

tips: Plant at least two for cross-pollination — self-fertility is unreliable.
The fruit doesn't store well — eat within a few days of harvest or freeze.
Hand-pollinate flowers for better fruit set — the flies and beetles that naturally pollinate them are often scarce in gardens.
Young plants need shade protection in the first 1–2 years.
warning: Some people develop skin reactions from handling the fruit skin or unripe fruit. Wear gloves when harvesting.

name: Prickly Pear Cactus
tagline: An edible cactus that produces sweet, colourful fruit. Hardy and drought-tolerant — needs almost nothing.

water.detail: Very drought-tolerant. Water occasionally in summer, barely at all in winter.

sun.detail: Needs maximum sun and heat.

soil.detail: Sandy or gravelly soil with perfect drainage. Rich soil causes rot.

temp.detail: Surprisingly cold-hardy. Some varieties survive harder frosts.

seedDepth.detail: Lay a pad on dry sandy compost — it roots easily. Seeds also work.

tips: Use thick gloves and tongs when handling — the tiny hair-like glochids are more irritating than the main spines.
Remove glochids from fruit by rolling in a thick towel or burning off with a flame before eating.
The pads (nopales) are also edible — a vegetable in Mexican cuisine.
One of the most drought-tolerant fruiting plants you can grow.
warning: The tiny hair-like spines (glochids) embed in skin and are extremely difficult to remove. Always use thick gloves and long tongs when handling.

name: Aronia (Chokeberry)
tagline: The most antioxidant-rich berry you can grow. Spectacular autumn colour and virtually maintenance-free.

water.detail: Water when establishing. Very drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade well. Full sun for best fruit production.

soil.detail: Remarkably adaptable — tolerates wet, dry, acidic, or alkaline conditions.

temp.detail: Extraordinarily cold-hardy.

seedDepth.value: Buy a plant or hardwood cuttings
seedDepth.detail: Hardwood cuttings root easily in autumn.

tips: The raw berries are very astringent — hence chokeberry. Much better cooked into juice, jam, or wine.
Spectacular autumn foliage makes it as valuable ornamentally as productively.
Self-fertile — one plant is sufficient.
One of the toughest, most low-maintenance fruiting shrubs available.
warning: Raw berries are very astringent and unpleasant. Always cook or process before eating.

name: Serviceberry
tagline: Beautiful spring blossom, sweet summer berries, and stunning autumn colour. One of the best garden trees.

water.detail: Water when establishing. Drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: Tolerates partial shade well — excellent for woodland gardens.

soil.detail: Most soils work well. Slightly acidic pH preferred.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root in autumn or winter.

tips: The berries taste like a cross between blueberry and almond — excellent eaten fresh.
Birds love serviceberries — net if you want any fruit for yourself.
One of the best small multi-season garden trees — spring blossom, summer fruit, autumn colour.
Self-fertile — one plant is sufficient.
warning: Birds will strip the berries very quickly. Net the tree as fruit starts to colour if you want any for yourself.

name: Cornelian Cherry
tagline: Early yellow flowers in February, then vivid red fruit in late summer. Beautiful and productive.

water.detail: Water when establishing. Very drought-tolerant once mature.

sun.detail: Grows well in sun or partial shade.

soil.detail: Very adaptable. Tolerates chalk, clay, and most conditions.

temp.detail: Very cold-hardy.

seedDepth.detail: Plant bare-root in autumn or winter.

care.harvest: August–September when fruit turns deep red

tips: One of the earliest flowering plants — vivid yellow flowers appear in February before the leaves.
The fruit is sour when fresh but excellent for jam, wine, and vodka infusions.
Self-fertile but produces better with a second plant nearby.
Very long-lived — some specimens in Europe are hundreds of years old.
warning: The fruit needs to be fully ripe (deep red) before eating — unripe cornelian cherries are unpleasantly astringent.
